title: Impulse as area: the force–time curve
scenario: A $0.5$ kg ball is struck by a bat: the contact force rises and falls as a brief pulse, $F(t) = F_{\max}\sin(\pi t/\tau)$ with $F_{\max} = 200$ N over $\tau = 0.02$ s. What impulse does it deliver, and how fast does the ball leave? Watch the impulse accumulate as the area under the force–time curve.

assumptions.claim: The contact force follows a half-sine pulse $F(t) = F_{\max}\sin(\pi t/\tau)$ (an idealized but realistic collision profile).
The struck object is free (the only horizontal force during contact is the strike), starting from rest.

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you
steps.latex: J = \bar F\,\Delta t \qquad J = \Delta p = m\,\Delta v
steps.prose: Impulse is the average force times the contact time, and it equals the change in momentum. But a real collision force is not constant — it rises and falls over a few milliseconds — so 'average force' hides where the number comes from.

steps.label: Impulse is the area under the force–time curve
steps.prose: Impulse is the integral of force over time — the area under the $F$–$t$ curve. For a constant force this is a rectangle ($\bar F\,\Delta t$); for a real pulse it is the area under the curve.

steps.label: Evaluate for the contact pulse $F(t) = F_{\max}\sin(\pi t/\tau)$
steps.prose: The area under a half-sine pulse is $\tfrac{2}{\pi}F_{\max}\tau$ — so the effective average force is $\tfrac{2}{\pi}F_{\max}\approx 0.64\,F_{\max}$. Its slope at any instant is the force $F(t)$.

steps.label: The change in momentum falls out of the same integral
steps.latex: \int F\,dt = \int m\,\frac{dv}{dt}\,dt = m\,\Delta v \ \Longrightarrow\ J = \Delta p
steps.prose: Because $F = m\,dv/dt$, the impulse integral is exactly $m\,\Delta v$. The impulse–momentum theorem is not a separate fact — it is the area under the force curve. Drag the cursor: the shaded area and the momentum change grow together.

proof.heading: The impulse is the area under the force–time curve — and it is exactly the change in momentum.
proof.detail: check $\tfrac{dJ}{dt} = F$; check $J = \int_0^t F\,dt'$; evaluate the pulse area $\tfrac{2}{\pi}F_{\max}\tau$; collapse the constant-force case to $F\,\Delta t$

checks.claim: The impulse's slope is the force: $J'(t) = F(t)$ — the area's rate of growth is the curve's height.
The accumulated impulse is the area: $J(t) = \int_0^t F\,dt'$.
The total impulse is $J = \tfrac{2}{\pi}F_{\max}\tau = \bar F\,\tau$ — exactly the area of the pulse.
For a constant force the integral collapses to $J = F\,\Delta t$ — the area is a rectangle (the quadrature).

misconception.claim: A bigger peak force always means a bigger change in momentum.
misconception.refuted_by: Not necessarily — the change in momentum is the *area* under the force–time curve (the impulse $\int F\,dt$), not the peak height. A small force acting over a long time delivers the same impulse — and the same $\Delta v$ — as a large force over a brief time. The area, not the height, is what changes the motion.

practice.asks: the change in velocity from the impulse
practice.prompt: A $0.5$ kg ball is struck by a force pulse that peaks at $F_{\max} = 200$ N over $\tau = 0.02$ s. By how much does its velocity change?

choices.misconception: Used $F_{\max}\tau$ for the impulse — a full rectangle. The force rises and falls, so its area (impulse) is less than peak times duration; here $J$ is the area under the pulse.
That is the impulse $J$ (in N·s). The velocity change is $\Delta v = J/m$ — divide by the mass.
